topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. I reread the subject line three times, my thumb hovering over the screen, before finally clicking into the body of the message. "Important Announcement Regarding [College Name] Operations." My eyes scanned the text, the familiar lines of campus life suddenly seeming distant and unreal. A slow, creeping cold started in my stomach. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and stared at the words repeating the closure date. I blinked, hard, trying to force the information to settle, but it just kept spinning, a dizzying kaleidoscope of disbelief. I pushed my glasses up my nose, the movement jerky, and then I stood up, pacing the small confines of my dorm room, my footsteps echoing loudly on the linoleum floor. "No," I whispered, the sound barely audible, tasting the word like ash.

[story 2]

It was the administrative office that delivered the news, a woman with an unnervingly calm demeanor and a stack of official-looking papers. When she finished speaking, the silence in the room felt thick, suffocating. I didn't move for a long moment. My hands, which had been gripping the edge of the desk, slowly relaxed, falling limp to my sides. I looked up at her, my gaze unfocused, as if I were trying to place the reality she was presenting into some known framework. My mouth went dry. "Wait," I managed, my voice thin and reedy. I shook my head once, sharply, a small, involuntary tremor running through my shoulders. The world seemed to tilt slightly; the fluorescent lights above flickered, and for a second, I couldn't reconcile the vibrant, bustling campus I’d known with this sudden, stark finality.

[story 3]

I found myself standing by the main quad, the autumn air suddenly feeling brittle and sharp against my skin. Everyone else seemed to be moving with a practiced, muted routine—a casual stroll, a quick chat—but I remained rooted to the spot, my backpack feeling impossibly heavy on my shoulders. I kept glancing toward the administration building, a knot tightening painfully in my chest. My breath hitched. I tried to call a friend, but my fingers wouldn't cooperate; they felt clumsy, disconnected. I looked down at my phone screen, then back up at the sprawling campus, and a strange, hollow sensation washed over me. It wasn't just sadness; it was a complete inability to process the scale of what was happening. I took a deep, ragged breath, the air catching in my throat, and slowly, deliberately, I turned away from the central gathering and started walking toward the edge of the property, needing distance from the undeniable fact.
